entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Read about world history from 1800-1900. View images and reference atlases and maps to set the people and events in their time and place. Update History Charts and Book of Centuries.

This topic is included in the following course(s): History: Grade 10 (Canada), History: Grades 11-12 (Canada), History: Grades 10-12 (U.S.)

rows.label: World History: Grades 10-12
rows.books: Blood and Iron: The Rise and Fall of the German Empire||When America First Met China||Erebus: The Story of a Ship

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: Blood and Iron: The Rise and Fall of the German Empire
Erebus: The Story of a Ship

blocks.text:
• Take a second to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

• If the previous passage is difficult to remember, try looking back at any chapter/section headings or illustrations or reading the first paragraph to jog the memory.

• Read the selection assigned OR read for the number of minutes allocated, leaving time for narration and mapwork. Use whichever guide (i.e., pages or minutes) works best for you. You want to read enough to be interesting, but not so much that you feel overwhelmed.

• If a book is challenging for you as a student, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently for a minute every now and then to make sure you are tracking. You might also want to read aloud to yourself. Or you might want to listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along with it. 

• If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! 

• Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your commonplace book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

• Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. 

• To keep yourself on track, you might want to set a timer, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

• After your narration, think about how what you read connects to other subjects, other books, and other things you've read, watched, or seen in the past or present. 

• Share any ideas that are particularly interesting to you with your fellow classmates, teachers, parents, friends, or others. You might even want to do a bit more reading or research on something on your own time in the afternoon or evenings.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

Those educating at home might pick one or two history lessons to read aloud with their students to keep up the habit of oral narration and foster conversation and relationship, and then simply give students these lesson plans and the rest of the books and check in with them throughout the term for conversation, support, and accountability. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete themselves silently and others read aloud and narrated together. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons, after everyone has narrated either together or independently in writing.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the most important actions taken when creating a new nation is establishing trade with other countries. As a newly founded nation, that is exactly what the United States of America set out to do. The United States traded for things like silk, gold and tea while other nations traded for everything from sea otter and seal pelts, to sandalwood, to opium, to cotton, and tortoiseshell. As the United States navigated new waters--both literally and figuratively--they would come into contact with a mighty trade nation that had a lot to offer them including the ever-important tea. However, China had it's own rules and regulations as well as it's own system of justice that was very different to the United States.
As you read through the chapter, I invite you to ponder what it must have been like to navigate these new waters and to meet new peoples whose way of life was so different from yours? What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? You will reflect on these questions after the reading.

➜ ANNOTATE
When America First Met China is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return too. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.146-160 - Stop at “exotic name 'bêche-de-mer',”

➜ MAP WORK
Familiarize yourself with the map at the beginning of When America First Met China. 

➜ WRITE
Answer the following questions in 3 paragraphs: What are the things you see in the text that surprise you? What things challenged your way of thinking? What things made you uncomfortable and why?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the ways we connect with history is through primary sources. A primary source is any first hand account history. These sources can be a journal, a map, a song, a picture and much more. A good historian will always rely heavily on primary sources when they are researching or writing history. Eric Jay Dolin has also relied heavily on these primary sources. As you through the next section, pay attention to the documents Eric Jay Dolin is mentioning. You will reflect on these primary sources after the reading.
For more about a primary source and secondary source, view the video provided in the extra helpings.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.161-176 - Start at “Bêche-De-Mer,” stop at "select promising opportunities."

➜ WRITE
Answer the following questions in two paragraphs: What are the primary sources Eric Jay Dolin is using to support his story telling? Why might these be good or bad primary sources to rely on?

➜ PONDER
What are some other books you've read that have used primary sources? Were they reliable? What makes a primary source unreliable?
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

links.text: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
In this chapter you will encounter a woman named Harriet Low who has recounted many of her adventures in journals. Harriet Low talked much about her time in an area of China that was a forbidden to women and her journals are insightful to anyone interested in history. Her journals are a primary source. 
Throughout the coming chapters, Eric Jay Dolin will rely heavily upon primary sources to help his story telling, while keeping it factual and correct. As you read through the next section, pay attention to the documents Eric Jay Dolin is mentioning.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.6 p.176-192 - Start at "The American Community" to the end of the chapter

➜ WRITE
Answer the question in one paragraph: aside from Harriet Low's journals, what are some other primary sources Eric Jay Dolin is using to support his story telling?
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ EXTRA HELPING
∞ Video Link: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

links.text: Primary vs. Secondary Sources: The Differences Explained

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
In Chapter 7, Eric Jay Dolin will explore China through American eyes. Specifically, he will explore how the nation came to be perceived as one with rampant property, horrible treatment of women, odd food and drink, and a violent government system that killed innocents. But where any of these things rooted in truth? Or just the ramblings of one people group encountering another nation with foreign eyes? It really didn't matter in the end. These people's writings influenced the perception of the nation of China. 

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.192-208

➜ WRITE
In three paragraphs, take time to reflect on what influenced the perception of the Chinese people. 

➜ PONDER 
How is your nation perceived?

lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ RECAP

➜ ANNOTATE

➜ READ, NARRATE & DISCUSS

• What did you learn your reading?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
A Historian named Ian Barrow wrote a short history of the East India Company which operated at the end of the sixteenth century all the way through the middle of the nineteenth. In his book he writes about the beginning of this company saying, "Toward the end of the sixteenth century, a group of English merchants based in London realized that there was an opportunity to make money in the spice trade." The British East India Company would exist for over 200 years and ended up becoming one of the largest monopolies on trade. Additionally, it was known for having it's own armies. The army at time reached 260,000 soldiers strong. 
The British government began to see the company for the threat it was and eliminated it's monopoly in 1833. From then on trade would look very different. The parliament would appoint people into government positions and these men would be faced with many challenges as they sought to build ties with China amid a growing conflict.
If you want to learn more or review the British East India Company check out the short video provided in the extra helpings.

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.223-242 start at "After the British" stop at "stage of the war."
lessons.teacherNotes:
➜ EXTRA HELPINGS
∞ Video Link: The East India Company || 400 Years: Britain & India || Episode 1

links.text: The East India Company || 400 Years: Britain & India || Episode 1

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
As you finish reading this chapter on the Opium War, I invite you to think on a few questions as you read though the rest of this chapter. Who was in the right? Who was in the wrong? How did each nation navigate this war? How do you think this impacted the trade relationship?

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.242-264 start at "This time the British" and finish at the end of the chapter.

➜ WRITE
In one paragraph, express how you see the participation of each nation mentioned in the chapter in the opium war. What surprised you about this chapter?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
How long do you wait for your packages? Perhaps if you and your family have fast shipping you'll only have to wait one or two days for your order to arrive at the doorstep. In the 1800s, it would take weeks for one ship to get to the other side of the world and pick up the items and then weeks to return. So, as one can imagine, there was a need to be the fastest ship out there in order to get the items back home and sold as quickly as possible. Many people took on the challenge of building these ships. 

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9 p.264- 277 end at "American invasion began."

➜ PONDER
Think on how shipping and trade has changed over the years.

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ INTRO
One of the horrible parts about reading through history is coming face-to-face with the reality of sinful humanity. John Fea writes, "Indeed, anyone who studies the past realizes that there are no heroes in history. While people may perform heroic acts, all humans are tainted by sin and are susceptible to acting in ways that preference themselves over others and God. Historians understand, perhaps better than most, the reality of the pain, suffering, injustice, anger, and vice brought on by sin. In other words, they understand the tragic dimensions of life." 
Exploitation is just one of the many injustices taking place in the 1800s. As you read through the chapter, you are allowed to feel heartbroken, angry and disappointed at the treatment of those who are also made in God's image. Both are belief in Christ and in the depravity of humanity can help us better navigate history. As John Fea writes, "Similarly, a belief in depravity helps us to better explain the human condition—the restlessness, the search for meaning, and the prideful ambition that has defined much of the past, especially in the modern era. Augustine was quite correct when he opened his Confessions with the famous words, “Our hearts are restless until they rest in you.”

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.9-10 p.277-290 start at "Ever since the" end at "the coolie trade"

➜ PONDER
Think more on Augustine's famous words, "Our hearts are restless until they rest in you."

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: When America First Met China

➜ CATCH UP
Catch up on any reading you've not completed.

➜ WRITE
If you have completed all the reading, I invite you to read the Epilogue and answer the following question in two paragraphs: How does reading the history of China's relationship with other nations inform our modern day interactions?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Erebus

➜ INTRO
What happens when nations finds themselves with war vessels, but no wars? For 1800s Britain, the answer was to send its ships on exploration missions. The Erebus was just one of the ships that made the transition from military vessel to a vessel used for research and exploration. The captains and crew of the vessel changed over the many years it spent on the seas.
In Erebus, you will read a book about a legendary ship. However, there is another benefit to Michael Palin’s book which makes it a helpful read as well as an enjoyable one. Erebus does not just share the story of the ship itself, but of the world in which this ship sailed. As you read through Erebus, take note of the names being mentioned in each chapter.
I also invite you to adventure with the people on Erebus and on the HMS terror. Pay attention to the letters and journals that are being mentioned and remember that these were real people who experienced a world very unlike our own. And yet, you’ll also see a world that is very familiar too.

➜ ANNOTATE
Erebus is a non-fiction book which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students will record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.1 p.1-16

➜ WRITE
Write two paragraphs about the world figures and the events that you’ve read about in chapter one of Erebus. Who has appeared in the chapter? Why are they important? What is the historical setting of the book?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Erebus

➜ INTRO 
Have you ever wondered what it was like to live on an exploration ship like the Erebus? This week's reading explores the lives of the many people who chose to embark on these voyages and how their lives looked day today. As you read through the section think about the many reasons that people to chose to take up these positions and what it was like for them on a day-to-day basis? Was the legacy of exploration worth it? 

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.4 p.49-61 stop at "the most poetic"

➜ WRITE
On p. 58-59, McCormick takes a horse and rides up to Napoleon's home and his final resting place. In one paragraph, describe the brief experience McCormick has on the island? Are there any clues as to how McCormick viewed Napoleon?

lessons.title: 30m World History: Grades 10-12 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Blood and Iron

➜ INTRO
In 1815 Napoleon was finally defeated after years spent conquering a continent and inspiring fear into his enemies. His fall would act as a catalyst in my many different fates, including the fate of Germany. Men like Otto von Bismarck would unify the Prussians, Bavarians, and Rhineland's under the German flag. From as early as 1815 the Germans would make a stand and the nation would become a world power.

➜ READ & NARRATE
p 15-35

➜ READ & NARRATE
